title: Design a Global Content Delivery Network
description: Learn how to cache content at the edge. Understand PoPs, Anycast routing, and cache hierarchies.

steps.detail: Drag an API Server onto the canvas. This is your source of truth for all content.
steps.hint: API Server is in the Compute section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect Client → API Server as the starting origin.

explanation.what: The server where original content lives. For dynamic content (user data, API responses) it's an application server. For static content (images, videos, CSS) it typically fetches from blob storage.
explanation.why: Even with a CDN, you need a canonical source. When a CDN edge node doesn't have a file cached (cache miss), it fetches from here — called an 'origin pull'. Origin only sees ~1-5% of total traffic after the CDN is warm.
explanation.tradeoff: Vulnerable to 'thundering herd' if CDN cache is flushed globally at once — every edge node simultaneously fetches the same content from origin, spiking load 100x.
explanation.realWorld: Netflix uses AWS S3 as origin for petabytes of video before it reaches their Open Connect CDN. Shopify's origin handles checkout (can't cache) while their CDN handles storefront pages (heavily cached).
explanation.capacity: Origin typically sized for 1-5% of peak traffic. If CDN absorbs 95%, a 100k RPS system only needs an origin sized for 5k RPS.

steps.detail: Drag a CDN node and connect it from the client. Users now hit the nearest edge node instead of your origin.
steps.hint: CDN is in the Networking section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect Client → CDN to route users through the edge network.

explanation.what: A network of globally distributed proxy servers (Points of Presence / PoPs) that cache your content close to users. Clients are directed to the nearest PoP via Anycast DNS routing.
explanation.why: Speed of light matters. A user in Tokyo hitting a US-East server experiences 150ms round-trip latency. A Tokyo CDN PoP serves them in <5ms. This is not just performance — it's revenue: 100ms latency = 1% conversion rate drop.
explanation.tradeoff: Cache invalidation is hard in a globally distributed system. When you update content, stale versions can persist at edge nodes for minutes or hours until TTLs expire. Instantaneous cache purges exist but cost extra.
explanation.realWorld: Cloudflare has 310+ PoPs across 100+ countries, handling ~20% of all HTTP traffic on the internet. CloudFront (AWS) serves 248 Tbps peak throughput. Fastly achieved 95%+ cache hit rates for Wikipedia globally.
explanation.capacity: Cloudflare: 100+ Tbps capacity. Single CDN PoP: 50-100 Gbps. Cache hit rate goal: 90%+ for static, 60-70% for dynamic content.

steps.detail: Connect a Load Balancer between CDN and the origin server. This collapses redundant origin fetches.
steps.hint: Load Balancer is in the Networking section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect CDN → Load Balancer to act as the origin shield.

explanation.what: A dedicated regional aggregation layer (also called a 'shield PoP' or 'mid-tier cache') that sits between edge nodes and origin. When 1000 edge nodes all miss the same content, only one request reaches origin — the shield handles the rest.
explanation.why: Without shielding, a viral event causes every edge node to simultaneously miss and fetch from origin — the thundering herd problem. A shield reduces origin requests by 99% for popular content.
explanation.tradeoff: Adds one extra hop for cache misses (edge → shield → origin). Cache miss latency increases by 10-30ms. Acceptable since misses are rare (<5% of requests) when the system is warm.
explanation.realWorld: Fastly's Shielding feature reduced origin traffic by 99% for one large media customer during a viral news event. Varnish Software's 'Grace Mode' serves stale content while fetching fresh, preventing even the shield from thundering to origin.
explanation.capacity: Shield absorbs 95-99% of cache misses. Origin sees 0.05-0.25% of total traffic in a well-shielded CDN.

steps.instruction: Add Blob Storage for Static Assets
steps.detail: Drag a Blob Storage node and connect it from the Load Balancer. Store images, videos, and static files here.
steps.hint: Blob Storage is in the Storage section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect Load Balancer → Blob Storage for static asset serving.

explanation.what: Immutable object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) for large binary files: images, videos, JavaScript bundles, CSS files. Designed for 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability with horizontal scale.
explanation.why: Blob storage is cheaper than serving static files from application servers (10-100x cost difference) and scales to arbitrary file sizes. Large video files can't live on SSD-backed API servers — they need petabyte-scale object stores.
explanation.tradeoff: Blob storage has higher latency than application memory (50-200ms for first byte vs <1ms). CDN caching eliminates most of this — cold fetches hit blob storage, warm requests serve from CDN edge memory.
explanation.realWorld: Netflix stores 15+ petabytes of video on S3. When a new movie launches, their Open Connect CDN proactively replicates files to all PoPs — no user ever triggers an origin pull for popular titles. Instagram stores 100M+ photos daily on S3 with CDN fronting delivery.
explanation.capacity: S3 scales to 3,500 PUT/s and 5,500 GET/s per prefix (add more prefixes to scale beyond this). Max object size: 5TB. Storage cost: ~$0.023/GB/month.

steps.detail: Drag a Monitoring node and connect it from the API Server. Track cache hit rates, origin load, and edge latency.
steps.hint: Monitoring is in the Observability section. Drag it onto the canvas, then connect API Server → Monitoring to track cache hit rates and latency.

explanation.what: Metrics tracking CDN performance: cache hit rate (target: 90%+), origin request volume, edge node p99 latency per region, cache purge propagation time, and bandwidth costs by PoP.
explanation.why: A 10% drop in cache hit rate can 10x your origin traffic and infrastructure bill. Monitoring catches stale cache-control headers, misconfigured TTLs, or suddenly dynamic content that shouldn't be cached.
explanation.tradeoff: CDN-level monitoring often requires vendor dashboards (Cloudflare Analytics, CloudFront metrics) plus your own RUM (Real User Monitoring) telemetry. Two different data sources with different aggregation semantics.
explanation.realWorld: Cloudflare provides per-PoP analytics showing cache hit rates, threats blocked, and bandwidth saved. Netflix monitors Open Connect performance with custom tooling — any PoP below 95% hit rate triggers an automated pre-warming job.
explanation.capacity: Monitoring target metrics: cache hit rate >90%, edge p99 latency <10ms, origin traffic <5% of total. Alert if origin traffic spikes >2x baseline — likely a cache invalidation storm or bug.

questions.question: What is 'Anycast' routing primarily used for in a CDN?

options.text: Broadcasting data to all servers simultaneously
Routing users to the geographically nearest server using the same IP address
Duplicating requests for redundancy across PoPs
Hiding the origin server's IP address from attackers

explanation.correct: Anycast assigns the same IP address to multiple servers globally. BGP routing protocol automatically sends a user's packets to the 'closest' PoP in network terms, minimizing latency without requiring DNS tricks or GeoIP lookups.

wrongAnswers.a: Broadcasting to all servers is 'Multicast' — completely different protocol and use case.
wrongAnswers.c: Anycast routes to exactly one destination (the nearest), not multiple. It doesn't duplicate requests.
wrongAnswers.d: Hiding origin IPs is a security benefit of CDNs, but Anycast's purpose is efficient routing, not IP masking.

questions.question: A CDN has a 95% cache hit rate for a system serving 1M RPS. How many requests per second reach your origin?

explanation.correct: 5% of 1M = 50,000 RPS reach origin. This means a 20x capacity multiplier — your origin only needs to handle 1/20th of total traffic. This is why CDNs are the single most cost-effective scaling lever for read-heavy systems.

wrongAnswers.a: 950,000 RPS would be a 5% hit rate (inverted). At 95% hit rate, CDN serves the vast majority.
wrongAnswers.c: 5,000 RPS would be a 99.5% hit rate — achievable but unusually high. 95% is more typical.
wrongAnswers.d: 1,000 RPS would require a 99.9% hit rate, which only very static content achieves.
